description: Förmedlar köp, försäljning eller uthyrning av hus, bostadsrätter, industribyggnader m.m. Marknadsför och visar objekt.
descriptionEnglish: Mediates the purchase, sale or letting of houses, flats, industrial buildings and similar property. Markets properties and conducts viewings.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that there will be competition for jobs as real estate agents over the next year. This means that people with the required education or experience will have limited to moderate opportunities for work, but the labour market may vary depending on specialisation within the occupation.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: Over a 2-year horizon, the Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that opportunities for work as real estate agents will be limited to moderate. Your chances of getting a job in this occupation increase if you have relevant certificates, authorisations, or vocational credentials.
forecast.ingress: There are about 4,900 employed real estate agents in Sweden, of whom around 51 percent are women and 49 percent are men.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Real estate brokers primarily perform knowledge- and communication-based work: valuing properties, preparing listings, marketing, matching buyers and sellers, scheduling and conducting viewings, handling documents, and advising clients during negotiations. Much of this is screen-based and language-heavy, so AI can substantially assist with drafting listings, lead qualification, market analysis, pricing suggestions, document review, and customer communication. However, the job also relies on trust, persuasion, local market knowledge, and in-person interactions such as property showings and client meetings, which limits full automation. In Sweden, regulation and professional practice further slow substitution, but the core task structure is still highly exposed to AI.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Current AI adoption for real estate agents in Sweden appears to be in the early-adoption range. There are clear observable signals of use in marketing content generation, ad copy drafting, image enhancement, lead handling, and scheduling/chat automation through CRM and property platform tools. Some larger brokerages and proptech vendors have started to integrate AI features into listing creation and customer communication workflows. However, there is little evidence that AI is yet a standard, deeply embedded part of everyday work across the occupation nationwide. Most usage still looks assistive and experimental rather than fully mainstream, with strong variation by employer size and digital maturity.

description:
Förvaltar fastigheter, svarar för uthyrning och besiktning samt skriver underhållsplaner. Förhandlar om hyror samt ansvarar för ekonomiska kalkyler och upprättar kontrakt. Tar in och granskar offerter.

Hit hör inte
Fastighetsingenjör (3112)
descriptionEnglish: Manages properties, is responsible for lettings and inspections, and draws up maintenance plans. Negotiates rents, is responsible for financial calculations, and prepares contracts. Obtains and reviews quotations. Does not include Property Engineer (3112).

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that there will be competition for jobs as property managers over the next year. This means that people with the required education or experience will have limited to moderate opportunities for work, but the labour market may vary depending on specialisation within the occupation.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: Over a 2-year horizon, the Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that opportunities for work as property managers will be limited to moderate. Your chances of getting a job in this occupation increase if you have relevant certificates, authorisations, or vocational credentials.
forecast.ingress: There are about 7,200 employed property managers in Sweden, of whom around 34 percent are women and 66 percent are men.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: The occupation is largely office-based and knowledge-intensive. Core tasks include property management, rental administration, inspections, maintenance planning, rent negotiations, financial calculations, contract drafting, and reviewing bids. These are all activities where AI can meaningfully assist with document generation, analysis, forecasting, contract support, and workflow automation. However, the role also includes tenant interaction, negotiations, on-site inspections, and coordination with contractors, which require human judgment and real-world context. Overall, the job is mostly cognitive/digital but still has important interpersonal and practical components, so exposure is high but not near-maximum.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Current AI adoption signals for property management are real but still mostly early-stage. In Sweden, fastighetsförvaltare increasingly have access to AI-enabled tools for tenant communication, email drafting, document summarization, contract review support, work order triage, and basic forecasting in property management systems. Larger real-estate firms and facility-management vendors are beginning to embed these functions, and job ads increasingly mention digital/AI competence in adjacent administrative and commercial property roles. However, for the core occupation, day-to-day work is still dominated by negotiation, site inspections, vendor coordination, budgeting, and legal/operational judgment, which limits full automation. Observable deployment is therefore more than marginal but not yet mainstream across employers, so a mid-level early-adoption score is most justified.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: This occupation is mainly field-based and physically executed: refilling vending machines, emptying coin boxes and parking meters, and reading utility meters or checking TV licenses. Overall, the role has limited to moderate AI exposure, with most of the work remaining hands-on and context-dependent.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For SSYK 9629, current observable AI adoption signals are weak. As of early 2026 there are no strong signs of mainstream AI tools being actively used in these tasks in Sweden. Some adjacent technologies may exist, such as route optimization, handheld reporting apps, image-based meter reading, or basic anomaly detection in utility operations, but these are not clear signals of broad AI adoption for the occupation itself. Overall, this looks marginal: a few experimental or indirect uses, not established deployment.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that there will be competition for jobs as caretakers over the next year. scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: The occupation is mainly hands-on and service-oriented: collecting and sorting mail and packages, distributing items, carrying luggage, helping with moves, performing simple repairs, and handling tickets/coats. These tasks depend heavily on physical presence, movement, and interpersonal contact. AI can assist indirectly with scheduling, tracking, routing, or inventory management, but it cannot replace the core embodied and on-site parts of the job. Overall exposure is low to moderate, so a score of 3 is appropriate.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Current AI adoption for this occupation appears marginal. These tasks do not currently show strong observable AI deployment signals in Sweden as of early 2026. While some adjacent tools may be used indirectly, such as digital scheduling, barcode sorting support, or basic service chatbots at larger facilities, there is little evidence of mainstream AI specifically assisting vaktmästare/messengers/porters in day-to-day work. Any adoption is likely limited to isolated pilots or general workplace software rather than occupation-specific AI tooling.

description: Nedmonterar uttjänta fordon, maskiner, apparater m.m. för att återvinna delar och komponenter. Hämtar, tömmer och sorterar sopsäckar och kärl samt annat avfall och skrot. Sopar, spolar, plogar och sandar gator och vägar. Skottar snö samt röjer parker från löv.
descriptionEnglish: Dismantles end-of-life vehicles, machines, appliances and similar items to recover parts and components. Collects, empties and sorts bin bags, bins and other waste and scrap. Sweeps, washes, clears, ploughs and grits streets and roads. Shovels snow and clears leaves from parks.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that there will be little competition for jobs as sanitation and recycling workers over the next year. This means that people with the required education or experience will have good opportunities to find work, but the labor market may vary depending on the specialization within the occupation.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: Over a 2-year horizon, the Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that job opportunities for sanitation and recycling workers will be high. Job opportunities in the occupation increase if you have relevant certificates, authorizations, or professional credentials.
forecast.ingress: There are around 8,600 employed sanitation and recycling workers in Sweden, of whom about 11 percent are women and 89 percent are men.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: This occupation is overwhelmingly physical and outdoor-based. Core tasks include dismantling equipment, collecting and sorting waste, emptying containers, street sweeping, plowing, sanding, shoveling snow, and clearing leaves. AI can assist indirectly with route planning, scheduling, predictive maintenance, and sorting optimization, but it cannot perform the central embodied work. The job therefore has low theoretical AI exposure.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Current AI adoption signals for waste collection, recycling, street cleaning, snow removal, and similar manual municipal cleanup work are still marginal. In Sweden, there are some observable uses of technology such as route optimization, sensor-based waste bin monitoring, fleet telematics, and planning software, but these are not strong AI work-assistance signals for the occupation itself. The core tasks remain physical and operational, and there is little evidence of routine AI tools being directly used by workers in this occupation. Any AI use is mostly indirect and limited to pilot-level scheduling or logistics support rather than mainstream adoption.

description: Portionerar mat, tar emot betalning i kassa samt plockar disk och diskar inom restaurang eller storkök. Förbereder mat, sköljer, skalar och hackar grönsaker. Deltar i enklare matlagning. Ansvarar för frukostservering.
descriptionEnglish: Portions food, takes payment at the till, and clears and washes up in restaurants or institutional kitchens. Prepares food, rinses, peels and chops vegetables. Takes part in simple cooking. Is responsible for breakfast service.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that there will be competition for jobs as restaurant and café assistants over the next year. This means that people with the required education or experience will have limited to moderate opportunities to find work, but the labor market may vary depending on the specialization within the occupation.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: Over a 2-year horizon, the Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that job opportunities for restaurant and café assistants will be limited to moderate. Job opportunities in the occupation increase if you have relevant certificates, authorizations, or professional credentials.
forecast.ingress: The assessment for restaurant and café assistants is a combined assessment for café and pastry assistants, restaurant and kitchen assistants, and similar occupations. There are around 67,000 employed restaurant and café assistants in Sweden, of whom about 62 percent are women and 38 percent are men.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: This occupation is mostly hands-on and service-oriented: serving food, handling dishes, basic food preparation, and breakfast service. AI can assist indirectly with scheduling, inventory, ordering, or simple training guidance, and possibly with cash register support, but it cannot replace the core physical and on-site tasks. The job has some routine cognitive components, yet the majority of work depends on real-world interaction and manual activity, so theoretical AI exposure is low to moderate.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale:
For SSYK 9412, there are some observable AI signals in the wider food service sector, but current adoption remains limited and uneven. The main AI-related use cases today are indirect: ordering and cashier systems with basic automation, demand forecasting in chain restaurants, scheduling/shift planning tools, and a few front-of-house assistants for reservations or customer chat. Some kitchen and cafeteria operators are also experimenting with AI-supported menu planning, waste reduction, and procurement, especially in larger private chains.

However, the core tasks in this occupation—portioning food, simple prep, dishwashing, breakfast service, and routine counter work—are still overwhelmingly manual and physically executed. There is little evidence of broad deployment of AI tools directly used by restaurant and kitchen assistants themselves in Swedish workplaces. Most adoption is at the restaurant-operations level rather than at the worker-task level, and public-sector or municipal kitchen environments are generally slower to adopt.

Overall, this looks like early adoption with some real pilots and scattered deployment, but not yet mainstream for the occupation itself.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that there will be competition for jobs as hand packers over the next year. scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: This occupation is overwhelmingly manual and physical: filling, sealing, labeling, packing, weighing, sorting, handling, and cleaning products and materials. AI can assist indirectly through automation planning, quality control, or machine vision in some factory settings, but the core tasks depend on embodied, on-site work rather than screen-based cognitive work. scores.currentAdoptionRationale: There are observable AI-related signals in manufacturing and packaging, but for hand packers and similar factory workers the adoption is still mostly early and uneven. For the occupation described here, the core tasks—manual filling, sealing, labeling, weighing, sorting, and cleaning—remain largely physically manual and only lightly assisted by software. Current adoption therefore appears to be at an early-stage level: real tools exist and some employers deploy them, but they are not yet mainstream for this occupation.

scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For this occupation there are very few strong observable AI adoption signals today. Overall, current adoption is marginal, not yet an established workplace norm.

scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For deckhands and able seamen, there are some observable AI-related signals in the maritime sector, but they are still limited and not yet mainstream for this occupation. Current deployment is mostly indirect: voyage planning, route optimization, predictive maintenance, and fleet operations software may include AI features, but these are typically used by ship operators, officers, or shore-based management rather than by matroser/jungman roles themselves. Onboard work such as lookout, maneuvering under instruction, deck maintenance, cleaning, and painting is largely manual and safety-critical, which reduces the scope for direct AI adoption. In Sweden, there may be pilot use of computer vision, sensor-based monitoring, or decision support on larger vessels, but there is little evidence of broad occupational-level AI tooling for this job as of early 2026. scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Truck operators mainly perform physically embodied work: driving a forklift, lifting, loading, unloading, and stacking goods in warehouses or terminals. Since the job is overwhelmingly hands-on and not screen-based, theoretical AI exposure is very low.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For truck operators (forklift drivers / material handling vehicle operators), there are only limited current AI adoption signals as of early 2026. Some warehouses and logistics sites use AI-enabled fleet management, route optimization, camera-based safety systems, and automated assist features in forklifts or adjacent material-handling equipment, but these are usually embedded in broader automation rather than direct AI tools used by the worker. The occupation remains highly manual and operational, and there is little evidence of widespread employer adoption of standalone AI workflows for this role. In Sweden, most observable AI usage in this area appears to be pilot-level or indirect, concentrated in larger logistics firms and highly automated distribution centers rather than mainstream across the occupation.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: This occupation is overwhelmingly hands-on and physically embodied. The core tasks involve driving and operating agricultural and forestry machinery in real outdoor environments, including ploughing, harrowing, sowing, harvesting, thinning, and final felling. AI can provide some support through route planning, precision agriculture, maintenance diagnostics, or optimization of machine settings, but it cannot replace the central real-world operation, terrain judgment, or safety-critical on-site control. scores.currentAdoptionRationale:
Current AI adoption for operators of agricultural and forestry machinery appears marginal to early-stage rather than mainstream. There are observable signals of AI in the broader sector, such as precision agriculture platforms, machine-vision weed detection, route optimization, yield analytics, and some autonomous or semi-autonomous machine functions. Forestry equipment vendors also market advanced assistance systems, sensor fusion, and predictive maintenance features. However, these are mostly embedded equipment capabilities or pilot deployments, not widely documented day-to-day AI tools used directly by machine operators in Sweden.

For this occupation, the work is highly physical, field-based, and strongly tied to specialized machinery and environmental conditions, which limits direct use of standalone AI software. Most adoption is likely occurring through OEM-provided automation and decision-support features rather than explicit employer-driven AI workflows. There are no strong signals of widespread AI tool use in job ads or routine operations specific to this occupation. Therefore, the best fit is a low score indicating a few pilots and emerging tooling, but not broad deployment.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Taxi drivers perform a mix of physical driving, passenger interaction, navigation, and situational judgment. scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Current AI adoption signals for taxi and passenger drivers in Sweden are still marginal. For the occupation itself, there is little evidence of widespread employer-led AI tooling adoption, and driving tasks remain largely manual. Overall, this looks like a few pilots and platform-level implementations rather than mainstream workplace adoption.

description: Växlar och iordningställer tåg på bangårdar samt säkerhetssynar och provbromsar dem. Sköter vagnupptagningen, rangeringen, vagnexpediering och godshantering. Utför enklare underhåll av fordon.
descriptionEnglish: Shunts and prepares trains in marshalling yards and carries out safety inspections and brake tests. Handles wagon marshalling, shunting, wagon dispatching and goods handling. Carries out simple vehicle maintenance.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: No forecast available.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: No forecast available.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Bangårdspersonal work is mainly hands-on and location-specific: switching and assembling trains in rail yards, safety inspection, brake tests, wagon handling, cargo handling, and minor vehicle maintenance. AI can assist with scheduling, documentation, and decision support, but the core tasks require physical presence, coordination with equipment and rail operations, and real-world safety judgment. Because the job is mostly embodied and operational rather than screen-based, theoretical AI exposure is low.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For Bangårdspersonal, there are some observable AI-related signals in rail operations, but they are still limited and not yet mainstream. The role is highly physical, safety-critical, and tightly procedure-driven, which makes direct AI deployment harder than in office-based work. Current adoption is mostly indirect: train operators and rail infrastructure firms may use AI for predictive maintenance, traffic planning, camera-based inspection support, scheduling optimization, and incident detection, but these tools are typically used by engineering, operations control, or maintenance teams rather than by yard staff themselves. There is little evidence of widespread AI use for the core hands-on tasks in shunting, coupling, brake testing, wagon handling, or basic vehicle maintenance. In Sweden, rail and public-sector-adjacent environments also tend to adopt new tooling cautiously due to safety, union, and regulatory constraints. Overall, the occupation shows marginal to early adoption signals, not significant current AI integration.

description: Framför lok, motorvagnar, pendeltåg, tunnelbana och växlingslok i person- och godstrafik.
descriptionEnglish: Drives locomotives, multiple units, commuter trains, underground trains and shunting locomotives in passenger and freight traffic.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that competition for jobs as train drivers will be low over the next year. This means that people with the required education or experience will have good opportunities for work, but the labor market may vary depending on the specialization within the occupation.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: Over a 2-year horizon, the Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that employment opportunities for train drivers will be good. Opportunities in the occupation increase if you have relevant certificates, authorizations, or occupational credentials.
forecast.ingress: There are about 4,800 employed train drivers in Sweden, of whom about 17 percent are women and 83 percent are men.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Train drivers perform a highly regulated, safety-critical, and strongly embodied job. The core work is operating rolling stock in real time, monitoring signals and track conditions, handling departures, arrivals, shunting, and responding to unexpected events. AI can assist with scheduling, route optimization, predictive maintenance, and some decision support, but it cannot take over the main physical and situational responsibilities of driving trains. In Sweden, strict safety rules, certification requirements, and collective agreements further limit direct substitution. Overall, the occupation has some AI exposure through adjacent digital support tasks, but the core task structure remains mostly operational and context-dependent.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For locomotive drivers in Sweden, there are currently only weak observable signs of direct AI adoption in the day-to-day occupation. The core work is safety-critical, highly regulated, and tied to certified procedures and human responsibility, which limits rapid deployment of AI decision support in cab operations. There are some adjacent digital systems in rail operations—such as predictive maintenance, traffic management, scheduling optimization, and monitoring in the broader railway sector—but these are not the same as AI tools actively used by most locomotive drivers themselves. As of early 2026, the evidence points more to isolated pilots or back-office optimization than mainstream AI use in this occupation, so the current adoption level is marginal.

description:
Monterar i seriemässig tillverkning större och mindre komponenter till maskiner, motorer och fordon genom att t.ex. skruva, svetsa, löda, nita eller limma.

Hit hör inte
Maskinmekaniker/Skogsmaskinmekaniker (7231)
Maskinmekaniker, industri/varvsindustri (7233)
Maskinmontör (7233)
descriptionEnglish: Assembles larger and smaller components for machines, engines and vehicles in series production, for example by screwing, welding, soldering, riveting or gluing. Does not include Machine Mechanic/Forestry Machine Mechanic (7231), Machine Mechanic, Industrial/Shipbuilding (7233) or Machine Fitter (7233).

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that there will be competition for jobs as assemblers in manufacturing over the next year. This means that people with the required education or experience will have small to medium opportunities for work, but the labor market may vary depending on the specialization within the occupation.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: Over a 2-year horizon, the Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that employment opportunities for assemblers in manufacturing will be small to medium. Opportunities in the occupation increase if you have relevant certificates, authorizations, or occupational credentials.
forecast.ingress: The assessment for assemblers in manufacturing is a combined assessment for vehicle assemblers; other assemblers; assemblers of metal, rubber and plastic products; assemblers of wood products; and assemblers of electrical and electronic equipment. There are about 51,500 employed assemblers in manufacturing in Sweden, of whom about 23 percent are women and 77 percent are men.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Fordonsmontörer perform mainly hands-on, physical assembly work in serial manufacturing: fastening, welding, soldering, riveting, and gluing components onto machines, engines, and vehicles. The core tasks require direct interaction with materials, tools, and production lines, so AI has limited ability to replace the embodied work itself. AI may support adjacent activities such as quality control, planning, instruction, or robot-assisted assembly, but the occupation remains primarily physical and on-site.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For vehicle assemblers (SSYK 8211), current AI adoption signals are limited and mostly indirect. In Swedish manufacturing, AI is more visibly deployed in planning, quality inspection, predictive maintenance, and robotics than in the core manual assembly role itself. For assemblers, the observable signals are mainly early-stage pilot uses such as AI-supported work instructions, vision-based quality checks, and production-line assistance in larger factories, rather than widespread day-to-day AI use by workers. Job-ad and employer signals for this occupation do not show strong mainstream AI tooling adoption comparable to office-based roles. Overall, this is marginal-to-early adoption rather than established practice.

nameEnglish: Assemblers of Electrical and Electronic Equipment
description:
Sätter i seriemässig tillverkning samman el- och teleutrustning genom att t.ex. skruva, löda och svetsa.

Hit hör inte
Elektromekaniker (7412)
Elektronikmontör (7420)
Installationselektriker (7411)
Linjemontör, elkraft (7413)
descriptionEnglish: Assembles electrical and telecommunications equipment in serial production, for example by screwing, soldering and welding. Does not include Electromechanic (7412), Electronics Assembler (7420), Installation Electrician (7411) or Power Line Installer (7413).

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that there will be competition for jobs as assemblers in manufacturing over the next year. This means that people with the required education or experience will have small to medium opportunities for work, but the labor market may vary depending on the specialization within the occupation.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: Over a 2-year horizon, the Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that employment opportunities for assemblers in manufacturing will be small to medium. Opportunities in the occupation increase if you have relevant certificates, authorizations, or occupational credentials.
forecast.ingress: The assessment for assemblers in manufacturing is a combined assessment for vehicle assemblers; other assemblers; assemblers of metal, rubber and plastic products; assemblers of wood products; and assemblers of electrical and electronic equipment. There are about 51,500 employed assemblers in manufacturing in Sweden, of whom about 23 percent are women and 77 percent are men.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: This occupation is mainly hands-on assembly work in serial production of electrical and telecommunication equipment, involving physical tasks such as screwing, soldering, and welding. AI can support related planning, quality control, machine vision, or process optimization, but the core job remains embodied and shop-floor based rather than screen-based. Because the task structure is mostly manual and dependent on real-world materials and equipment, theoretical AI exposure is low to moderate.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale:
For SSYK 8212 (assemblers of electrical and electronic equipment), there are some observable AI-related signals in manufacturing, but they are still limited and not yet mainstream for this occupation. In Swedish and Nordic factories, AI is more visibly adopted in adjacent areas such as predictive maintenance, quality inspection with computer vision, production planning, and robotics programming, rather than in the hands-on assembly tasks themselves. For this specific role, the work remains highly physical, repetitive, and process-driven, so current AI tools tend to support the surrounding production system instead of directly changing the core assembly job.

There are some early pilots and vendor offerings for AI-assisted visual inspection, work-instruction systems, and cobot/robot cell support, which can affect assemblers indirectly. However, as of early 2026, there is little evidence of widespread employer deployment of AI tools specifically used by electrical/electronic assemblers in Sweden. This makes the occupation best characterized as marginal early adoption rather than significant current adoption.

nameEnglish: Power Plant and Waterworks Plant Operators
description: Styr och övervakar anläggningar och maskiner som framställer och distribuerar elektricitet, ånga och hetvatten. Styr anläggningar för förbränning av avfall samt för upptagning, rening och distribution av vatten. Styr kyl- och ventilationsmaskineri.
descriptionEnglish: Controls and monitors plant and machinery that generate and distribute electricity, steam and hot water. Controls incineration plants and plants for the extraction, treatment and distribution of water. Operates refrigeration and ventilation machinery.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that competition for jobs as plant operators at heating and waterworks will be very low over the next year. This means that people with the required education or experience will have very good opportunities for work, but the labor market may vary depending on the specialization within the occupation.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: Over a 2-year horizon, the Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that employment opportunities for plant operators at heating and waterworks will be very good. Opportunities in the occupation increase if you have relevant certificates, authorizations, or occupational credentials.
forecast.ingress: There are about 8,900 employed plant operators at heating and waterworks in Sweden, of whom about 9 percent are women and 91 percent are men.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: This occupation is a mixed control-room and field operations role. A substantial part of the work involves monitoring systems, reading instruments, logging conditions, and making operational decisions based on process data, which AI can support through anomaly detection, predictive maintenance, optimization, and alarm triage. However, the job also includes hands-on oversight of physical plants, responding to equipment faults, coordinating on-site interventions, and ensuring safe operation of water, heat, and ventilation infrastructure, which requires real-world embodiment and accountability. Because the core tasks are split between digital supervision and physical plant operations, exposure is moderate rather than high.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Current AI adoption signals for this occupation appear marginal. In Swedish heat, water, and utility operations, the observable use of AI is still limited to adjacent functions such as predictive maintenance, anomaly detection in SCADA/industrial monitoring, and optimization pilots in larger utilities or equipment vendors. However, there is little evidence that AI is broadly embedded in the daily work of plant operators/drifttekniker themselves, especially for routine control-room supervision, manual rounds, alarms, and compliance tasks. Most deployments remain pilot-scale, vendor-led, or part of broader digitalization rather than standard workplace tooling. This fits a low current adoption level: some experimentation exists, but widespread operational use is not yet visible.

AI is more commonly seen in adjacent areas such as predictive maintenance, anomaly detection, quality inspection, energy optimization, and advanced analytics at plant level, not as a routine day-to-day tool directly used by this occupation.

As of early 2026, there are limited signs of direct AI deployment for the role itself: a few pilot projects, vendor offerings, and larger-company initiatives, but not clear mainstream use across employers. The occupation is highly safety-critical and physically tied to furnace and metallurgical processes, which tends to slow rapid AI substitution or augmentation. Overall, the current adoption signal is low, corresponding to a small number of pilots and early experiments rather than broad operational use.

nameEnglish: Packaging, Bottling and Labelling Machine Operators
description:
Styr och övervakar maskiner som tappar på flaskor, försluter, emballerar, packar, väger samt märker och fäster fraktsedlar m.m. på produkter, förpackningar och behållare.
 
Hit hör inte
Handpaketerare (9320)
Varumärkare (9320)
descriptionEnglish: Controls and monitors machines that fill bottles, seal, wrap, pack, weigh, label and attach waybills to products, packages and containers. Does not include Hand Packer (9320) or Marking Worker (9320).

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that competition for jobs as machine operators in manufacturing will be low over the next year. This means that people with the required education or experience will have good opportunities for work, but the labor market may vary depending on the specialization within the occupation.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: Over a 2-year horizon, the Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that employment opportunities for machine operators in manufacturing will be good. Opportunities in the occupation increase if you have relevant certificates, authorizations, or occupational credentials.
forecast.ingress: The assessment for machine operators in manufacturing is a combined assessment for machine operators in cement, stone and concrete products; machine operators in the meat and fish processing industry; machine operators in the plastics industry; operators in sawmills, planing mills and plywood manufacturing, etc.; machine operators in chemical, technical and photographic products; machine operators in the paper products industry; other machine operators in the textile, skin and leather industry; machine operators in dairies; other machine operators in the food industry, etc.; machine operators in the rubber industry; machine operators in the milling, bakery and confectionery industry; machine operators in surface treatment; rolling mill operators; machine setters and machine operators in metalworking; machine carpenters and machine operators in the wood industry; machine operators in wood surface treatment; machine operators in bleaching, dyeing and washing; machine operators in filling, packing and labelling; manual wood surface finishers; and machine operators in pharmaceutical products. There are about 115,000 employed machine operators in manufacturing in Sweden, of whom about 21 percent are women and 79 percent are men.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: This occupation is mainly hands-on and machine-oriented rather than screen-based. Workers monitor and adjust packaging, filling, sealing, weighing, labeling, and dispatch-label attachment equipment, which involves physical presence on the production line and direct interaction with machinery and materials. AI can assist with monitoring, predictive maintenance, quality control, and process optimization, but it is less likely to replace the core embodied tasks. Because the job includes some technical oversight and decision-making, exposure is not minimal, but it remains mostly physical and operational.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Current AI adoption signals for machine operators in filling, packing, and labeling are limited and mostly indirect. In Sweden, there are observable deployments of computer vision, sensor-based quality control, predictive maintenance, and some automated packaging/labeling lines, but these are usually embedded in machinery rather than used as clear, operator-facing AI tools. For this occupation, most current work still centers on supervising conventional automated equipment, troubleshooting, and manual adjustments. There are some early pilots and vendor-driven upgrades in food, pharma, and logistics packaging environments, but AI is not yet a mainstream, routine tool used by the majority of employers in this role. Overall, this is best rated as early adoption with some experimentation rather than broad deployment.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: This occupation is overwhelmingly manual and physically embodied: applying paint or varnish to wood or wood-based panels. scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For manual wood surface finishers, there are currently only weak observable AI adoption signals. In Sweden, the strongest AI adoption signals are in office, service, and knowledge work; for this occupation there is little evidence of widespread employer adoption of standalone AI workflows. forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that competition for jobs for process operators in manufacturing will be low over the next year. forecast.ingress: The assessment for process operators in manufacturing is a combined assessment for process supervisors in metal production; process operators in pulp; process operators in crushing and ore processing; and process operators in paper. scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: This occupation is centered on operating and monitoring industrial paper-production and finishing machinery. There is some digital/cognitive work involved in controlling systems, adjusting parameters, checking process data, and responding to alarms, which AI could support through predictive maintenance, process optimization, and anomaly detection. However, the core work remains strongly tied to physical equipment, real-time plant conditions, safety procedures, and hands-on intervention in a manufacturing environment. Because the job is mostly machine-operation and on-site oversight rather than screen-based knowledge work, the overall theoretical AI exposure is moderate-low.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For paper machine operators and finishing operators in the Swedish pulp and paper industry, there are some observable AI-adjacent signals, but they remain limited. Current deployments are more often advanced automation, process control, sensor analytics, and predictive maintenance than explicit AI tools used directly by operators in day-to-day work. Some mills may use machine learning for quality monitoring, anomaly detection, energy optimization, or maintenance planning, but this is typically handled by engineers, maintenance teams, or centralized operations systems rather than being a mainstream operator-facing workflow. In job ads and industry reporting, there is little evidence of widespread AI-specific tool adoption for this occupation in Sweden as of early 2026. Overall, this looks like marginal-to-early adoption: some pilots and selective industrial use, but not broad, clearly visible AI usage by process operators themselves.

description:
Styr och övervakar maskiner som krossar, maler, blandar och på andra sätt bearbetar säd, kryddor och liknande råvaror för konsumtion eller djurfoder. Styr och övervakar maskiner och linjer som bereder deg samt formar och bakar bröd, bakverk, pasta, choklad och konfektyr.

Hit hör inte
Bagare/Konditor (7612)
descriptionEnglish: Controls and monitors machines that crush, grind, mix and otherwise process grain, spices and similar raw materials for human consumption or animal feed. Controls and monitors machines and lines that prepare dough and shape and bake bread, pastries, pasta, chocolate and confectionery. Does not include Baker/Pastry Chef (7612).

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that competition for jobs for machine operators in manufacturing will be low over the next year. This means that people with the required education or experience will have strong opportunities for work, but the labor market may vary depending on the specialization within the occupation.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: Over a two-year horizon, the Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that opportunities for work for machine operators in manufacturing will be great. The chances of finding work in the occupation increase if you have relevant certificates, authorizations, or vocational credentials.
forecast.ingress: The assessment for machine operators in manufacturing is a combined assessment for machine operators in cement, stone and concrete products; machine operators in the meat and fish processing industry; machine operators in the plastics industry; operators in sawmills, planing mills and plywood manufacturing, etc.; machine operators in chemical-technical and photographic products; machine operators in the paper goods industry; other machine operators in the textile, fur and leather industry; machine operators in dairies; other machine operators in the food industry, etc.; machine operators in the rubber industry; machine operators in the flour, bakery and confectionery industry; machine operators in surface treatment; rolling mill operators; machine setters and machine operators in metalworking; machine carpenters and machine operators in the wood industry; machine operators in wood surface treatment; machine operators in bleaching, dyeing and washing; machine operators in filling, packing and labeling; manual surface finishers in wood; and machine operators in pharmaceutical products. There are about 115,000 people working as machine operators in manufacturing in Sweden, of whom about 21 percent are women and 79 percent are men.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: This occupation is primarily hands-on and production-floor based: operating, monitoring, and adjusting milling and bakery/confectionery machines, handling raw materials, and responding to equipment issues in real time. AI can support parts of the work, such as predictive maintenance, process optimization, quality control via sensors and vision systems, and production scheduling, but it cannot replace the physical supervision, machine intervention, hygiene compliance, and immediate response required on the line. Overall, the role has some technological exposure but remains mostly embodied and operational, so AI exposure is moderate-low.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Current AI adoption signals for machine operators in milling, bakery, and confectionery are still limited and mostly indirect. In 2026, the strongest observable AI use in this occupation is likely around adjacent production systems rather than the operator role itself: predictive maintenance, computer vision for quality control, process optimization, and automated line monitoring. These tools are increasingly available in food manufacturing, but they are typically implemented at plant or engineering level, not as day-to-day AI tools used directly by operators. For the operator task profile described here—controlling and monitoring machines and lines—there is little evidence of widespread AI-assisted tooling comparable to office or knowledge-work occupations. Swedish food manufacturing is also relatively conservative in frontline digitization, with adoption constrained by legacy equipment, food-safety requirements, and cost sensitivity. Overall this points to early adoption rather than mainstream deployment: some employers and sites are experimenting, but current observable AI use among these workers remains modest.

I do not see strong evidence of mainstream AI use specifically by machine operators in this occupation in Sweden as of early 2026. I do not see strong evidence of widespread AI tools being actively used by employers in this exact role, such as in job ads, vendor case studies, or public Swedish adoption examples. There is no strong evidence of widespread AI tools being actively used by operators in day-to-day tasks in Sweden, and job-ad signals for this type of role are typically about automation competence rather than generative AI. So the current adoption level looks marginal rather than mainstream.

description:
Styr och övervakar maskiner och utrustning som putsar, pläterar eller ytbehandlar metall eller metallföremål i syfte att öka motståndskraften mot frätning och förslitning, för dekorativa ändamål eller för att ge metall elektriska eller magnetiska egenskaper.

Hit hör inte
Lackerare (7132)
Träförgyllare (7521)
descriptionEnglish: Operate and monitor machines and equipment that polish, plate or finish metal or metal objects in order to increase resistance to corrosion and wear, for decorative purposes, or to give metal electrical or magnetic properties. Not including: painter/varnisher (7132); wood gilder (7521).

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that competition for jobs for machine operators in manufacturing will be low over the next year. This means that people with the required education or experience will have strong opportunities for work, but the labor market may vary depending on the specialization within the occupation.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: Over a two-year horizon, the Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that opportunities for work for machine operators in manufacturing will be great. The chances of finding work in the occupation increase if you have relevant certificates, authorizations, or vocational credentials.
forecast.ingress: The assessment for machine operators in manufacturing is a combined assessment for machine operators in cement, stone and concrete products; machine operators in the meat and fish processing industry; machine operators in the plastics industry; operators in sawmills, planing mills and plywood manufacturing, etc.; machine operators in chemical-technical and photographic products; machine operators in the paper goods industry; other machine operators in the textile, fur and leather industry; machine operators in dairies; other machine operators in the food industry, etc.; machine operators in the rubber industry; machine operators in the flour, bakery and confectionery industry; machine operators in surface treatment; rolling mill operators; machine setters and machine operators in metalworking; machine carpenters and machine operators in the wood industry; machine operators in wood surface treatment; machine operators in bleaching, dyeing and washing; machine operators in filling, packing and labeling; manual surface finishers in wood; and machine operators in pharmaceutical products. There are about 115,000 people working as machine operators in manufacturing in Sweden, of whom about 21 percent are women and 79 percent are men.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: This occupation is mainly hands-on and equipment-based: workers operate, monitor, and adjust surface-treatment machinery in a production setting. The core work involves physical presence, inspection of parts, handling materials, and responding to machine behavior, which limits direct AI substitution. AI can still assist with scheduling, quality control, predictive maintenance, process optimization, and documentation, but it does not replace the central embodied task structure. Overall exposure is low to moderate, with some cognitive support potential but a predominantly manual industrial role.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Current AI adoption signals for surface-treatment machine operators are marginal. In early 2026, there are some observable uses of AI-enabled features in adjacent industrial environments—such as predictive maintenance, visual quality inspection, anomaly detection, and production monitoring—but these are typically deployed at the plant or line level rather than as direct, routine tools used by operators in this occupation. For SSYK 8121 specifically, there is little evidence of widespread, role-specific AI tooling in Swedish job ads, employer communications, or public-facing implementation examples. Most workplaces still rely on conventional automation, PLC/SCADA systems, and standard process control rather than AI-assisted operator workflows. So adoption exists in pockets, but it is not yet mainstream for this occupation.

forecast.ingress: The assessment for machine operators in manufacturing is a combined assessment for machine operators in cement, stone and concrete products; machine operators in the meat and fish processing industry; machine operators in the plastics industry; operators in sawmills, planing mills and plywood manufacturing, etc.; machine operators in chemical-technical and photographic products; machine operators in the paper goods industry; other machine operators in the textile, fur and leather industry; machine operators in dairies; other machine operators in the food industry, etc.; machine operators in the rubber industry; machine operators in the flour, bakery and confectionery industry; machine operators in surface treatment; rolling mill operators; machine setters and machine operators in metalworking; machine carpenters and machine operators in the wood industry; machine operators in wood surface treatment; machine operators in bleaching, dyeing and washing; machine operators in filling, packing and labeling; manual surface finishers in wood; and machine operators in pharmaceutical products. scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Valsverksoperatörer mainly perform hands-on industrial work: controlling and monitoring rolling mills, adjusting machinery, and responding to process conditions in a physical production environment. description: Borrar och transporterar malm från gruva. Borrar och förstärker berg vid anläggningsarbeten.
descriptionEnglish: Drill and transport ore from mines. Drill and reinforce rock in construction work.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that competition for jobs for mining and quarrying workers will be very low over the next year. This means that people with the required education or experience will have very good opportunities for work, but the labour market may vary depending on specialization within the occupation.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: Over a two-year horizon, the Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that the opportunities for work for mining and quarrying workers will be very good. Job prospects in the occupation improve if you have relevant certificates, authorizations, or occupational credentials.
forecast.ingress: The assessment for mining and quarrying workers is an overall assessment covering mining and quarrying workers, rock blasters, stonecutters, etc. There are about 4,400 employed mining and quarrying workers in Sweden, of whom about 4 percent are women and 96 percent are men.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: This occupation is almost entirely physical and site-based: drilling, moving ore, and reinforcing rock in mines and construction settings. AI may assist indirectly with planning, monitoring, equipment maintenance, or safety analysis, but the core work depends on embodied labor, heavy machinery operation, and real-world conditions that AI cannot easily replace. Under the rubric, this is a very low theoretical exposure role.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Current AI adoption signals for miners and quarry workers are still early but real. In Sweden and other Nordic mining operations, there are observable deployments of AI-enabled systems for fleet optimization, predictive maintenance, drill planning, geotechnical monitoring, and remote operation of heavy equipment. These tools affect the work of mining and quarry workers indirectly and sometimes directly, especially in larger industrial sites. However, the core hands-on tasks in SSYK 8111—drilling, rock reinforcement, ore transport, and site work—remain primarily physical and machinery-based rather than AI-mediated. Most employers are not yet advertising AI use as a routine part of this occupation, and adoption is far less visible than in office, engineering, or control-room roles. So the current state is best characterized as early adoption with some meaningful industrial deployment, but not mainstream occupation-wide AI use.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: This occupation is primarily hands-on and process-control oriented in an industrial setting. scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Current AI adoption signals for process operators in rock crushing and ore beneficiation appear marginal. forecast.ingress: The assessment for machine operators in manufacturing is a combined assessment for machine operators in cement, stone and concrete products; machine operators in the meat and fish processing industry; machine operators in the plastics industry; operators in sawmills, planing mills and plywood manufacturing, etc.; machine operators in chemical-technical and photographic products; machine operators in the paper goods industry; other machine operators in the textile, fur and leather industry; machine operators in dairies; other machine operators in the food industry, etc.; machine operators in the rubber industry; machine operators in the flour, bakery and confectionery industry; machine operators in surface treatment; rolling mill operators; machine setters and machine operators in metalworking; machine carpenters and machine operators in the wood industry; machine operators in wood surface treatment; machine operators in bleaching, dyeing and washing; machine operators in filling, packing and labeling; manual surface finishers in wood; and machine operators in pharmaceutical products. There are about 115,000 people working as machine operators in manufacturing in Sweden, of whom about 21 percent are women and 79 percent are men.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: This occupation is primarily hands-on and machine- or plant-operating work in a physical production environment. The core tasks involve running and monitoring concrete/asphalt equipment, making molds, reinforcing, casting, and finishing materials. AI may help with predictive maintenance, quality control, scheduling, or process optimization, but it cannot replace the embodied, on-site nature of the work or the need to handle materials and machinery directly. Therefore the theoretical AI exposure is low.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Current AI adoption signals for this occupation appear marginal. In cement, stone, concrete, and asphalt production, there are observable uses of automation, sensors, predictive maintenance, quality control vision systems, and process optimization software, but these are generally not framed as direct AI tools used by operators in day-to-day work. The occupation is highly industrial and equipment-driven, so most digital change is coming through machine automation rather than visible generative AI adoption. In Swedish workplaces, AI uptake is rising overall, but for this specific role the strongest signals are limited to a few pilot-like or indirect uses in larger plants and equipment suppliers. There is little evidence of widespread, occupation-level AI tooling comparable to knowledge work roles.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: This occupation is dominated by hands-on craft work: measuring, cutting fabric, sewing, altering garments, and working with physical materials such as textiles and leather. There is also some cognitive/design work in drafting patterns and suggesting models, which AI can assist with, for example by generating design ideas, pattern recommendations, or customer-facing customization options. However, the core task remains highly embodied, skill-based, and dependent on manual execution and fitting to real bodies and materials. scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Current AI adoption for tailors and dressmakers in Sweden appears to be in the early-adoption phase rather than mainstream. There are observable signals of AI use in adjacent parts of the apparel workflow, especially in pattern generation, sizing, design ideation, fabric waste reduction, and e-commerce customization tools, but these are not yet broadly embedded in everyday tailoring and alteration work. In most small ateliers and alteration shops, the core work remains highly manual and customer-specific, which limits the immediate impact of AI. Some larger clothing manufacturers, fashion brands, and digital pattern-making vendors are beginning to use AI-enabled design and pattern tools, but the occupation itself shows only limited direct deployment so far. Overall, this points to some experimentation and selective uptake, not widespread adoption.

Job ads and employer signals for this occupation rarely require AI tools specifically, and adoption appears experimental rather than mainstream. Overall this fits marginal current adoption with a few pilots or scattered use cases.

description: Ställer in hel- och halvautomatiska träbearbetningsmaskiner samt utför bearbetning.
descriptionEnglish: Set up fully and semi-automatic wood-working machines and carry out machining work.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that competition for jobs for machine operators in manufacturing will be low over the next year. This means that people with the required education or experience will have very good opportunities for work, but the labour market may vary depending on specialization within the occupation.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: Over a two-year horizon, the Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that the opportunities for work for machine operators in manufacturing will be good. Job prospects in the occupation improve if you have relevant certificates, authorizations, or occupational credentials.
forecast.ingress: The assessment for machine operators in manufacturing is an overall assessment covering machine operators in cement, stone and concrete products; machine operators in the meat and fish processing industry; machine operators in the plastics industry; operators in sawmills, planing mills and plywood, etc.; machine operators in chemical-technical and photographic products; machine operators in the paper products industry; other machine operators in the textile, leather and hide industry; machine operators in dairies; other machine operators in the food industry, etc.; machine operators in the rubber industry; machine operators in the flour, bakery and confectionery industry; machine operators in surface treatment; rolling-mill operators; machine setters and machine operators in metal work; machine carpenters and machine operators in the wood industry; machine operators in surface treatment of wood; machine operators in bleaching, dyeing and washing; machine operators in filling, packing and labelling; manual surface finishers in wood; and machine operators in pharmaceutical products. There are about 115,000 employed machine operators in manufacturing in Sweden, of whom about 21 percent are women and 79 percent are men.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: This occupation is primarily hands-on and machine-based: workers set up and operate semi-automatic and automatic woodworking machines, monitor processing, and handle physical materials. AI could assist somewhat with machine setup, optimization, predictive maintenance, quality control, and scheduling, but the core work remains tied to operating real equipment in a workshop/industrial setting. Because the job is mostly physical and equipment-dependent rather than screen-based, theoretical AI exposure is low to moderate.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale:
Current AI adoption signals for machine woodworkers and machine operators in the wood industry are still limited and mostly at the early-experimentation stage. In Swedish wood-processing workplaces, the core tasks are operating, setting, and monitoring specialized industrial machinery, where automation already exists but AI-specific tooling is not yet widely visible in day-to-day use. Observable signals are more common in adjacent areas such as predictive maintenance, machine vision for quality inspection, production planning, and optimization of sawmill/manufacturing lines, rather than in direct operator assistance.

There are some current adoption indicators: larger industrial employers may use AI-enabled monitoring systems, and Swedish manufacturing firms increasingly mention AI in production, maintenance, or process optimization contexts. However, for this specific occupation, there is little evidence that AI tools are broadly deployed as standard practice across employers. Most jobs still rely on conventional CNC/automated machinery, procedural controls, and operator expertise, with AI appearing in a few pilots or selected facilities rather than mainstream deployment.

Overall, this points to early adoption but not significant widespread use.

There is little evidence of widespread employer adoption of AI copilots or generative AI directly integrated into the day-to-day tasks of this occupation. descriptionEnglish: Receive, quality-check, adapt and adjust materials prior to printing.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that there will be competition for jobs for printers and prepress technicians over the next year. This means that people with the required education or experience will have small to medium opportunities for work, but the labour market may vary depending on specialization within the occupation.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: Over a two-year horizon, the Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that the opportunities for work for printers and prepress technicians will be small to medium. Job prospects in the occupation improve if you have relevant certificates, authorizations, or occupational credentials.
forecast.ingress: The assessment for printers and prepress technicians is an overall assessment covering prepress technicians and printers. There are about 5,700 employed printers and prepress technicians in Sweden, of whom about 23 percent are women and 77 percent are men.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: Prepress technicians perform a mixed set of tasks: receiving materials, checking quality, adapting files and settings for printing, and making technical adjustments. The cognitive/digital parts of the role are fairly exposed to AI, especially image/file inspection, layout correction, color and print-preparation checks, and workflow automation. However, the job also includes hands-on handling of physical materials, coordination with print production, and context-specific judgment about print readiness that cannot be fully automated. Overall, this is a mid-level exposure occupation with meaningful AI assistance potential but not a fully screen-based role.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale:
There are some observable AI adoption signals for prepress technicians, but they appear to be early-stage rather than mainstream. In current print/prepress workflows, AI is increasingly available in software used for layout checking, image enhancement, color correction support, defect detection, PDF preflight, and automated file preparation. These tools can reduce manual inspection work and speed up routine adjustments, which makes the occupation exposed to AI-enabled assistance.

However, the evidence for broad, routine deployment specifically among Swedish prepress employers is still limited. The role remains strongly tied to production-specific judgment, quality control, customer requirements, and handling varied input files, which means many workplaces likely still rely on conventional prepress software and human review. Compared with occupations where AI is clearly embedded in day-to-day workflows, this looks like an early adoption case: some firms are experimenting or using AI-enabled features, but it is not yet a dominant, standard practice across the occupation in Sweden.

descriptionEnglish: Make, install, adjust and repair technical and scientific precision instruments. Make and repair locks and weapons. Adjust and repair aids and equipment for people with disabilities. Fit and service locks and access control systems, pick locks and make keys. Repair, clean, adjust and sell watches.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that competition for jobs for precision mechanics will be low over the next year. This means that people with the required education or experience will have very good opportunities for work, but the labour market may vary depending on specialization within the occupation.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: Over a two-year horizon, the Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that the opportunities for work for precision mechanics will be good. Job prospects in the occupation improve if you have relevant certificates, authorizations, or occupational credentials.
forecast.ingress: There are about 3,100 employed precision mechanics in Sweden, of whom about 9 percent are women and 91 percent are men.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: This occupation is primarily hands-on and mechanical: manufacturing, adjusting, repairing, unlocking, and servicing precision instruments, locks, weapons, disability aids, and watches. AI can assist with diagnostics, parts identification, scheduling, documentation, customer communication, and troubleshooting guidance, but it cannot replace the core physical work, manual dexterity, on-site installation, or real-world repair tasks. Overall exposure is limited to support functions, so the occupation is mostly low-to-moderate AI exposed.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Current AI adoption signals for finmekaniker are very limited. The occupation is highly hands-on, centered on precision mechanical work such as repairing watches, locks, weapons, and assistive devices, where the core tasks are physical inspection, adjustment, assembly, and repair rather than digital knowledge work. As of early 2026, there are no strong observable signs of mainstream AI use in this occupation in Sweden, and I do not see evidence of widespread AI-enabled tools being embedded in daily workflows. Some adjacent support functions may use AI indirectly for scheduling, customer communication, inventory, or documentation, but that does not indicate meaningful AI adoption in the core occupation. Overall this fits a marginal adoption level with only limited experimentation or indirect support use.

description: Tillverkar, justerar och reparerar formverktyg för plast- eller metallprodukter samt klipp- och bockverktyg. Utför verktygsfräsning, verktygssvarvning och gnistbearbetning samt bänkarbete.
descriptionEnglish: Make, adjust and repair mould tools for plastic or metal products as well as cutting and bending tools. Carry out tool milling, tool turning and spark erosion, and bench work.

forecast.shortTermOutlook: No forecast available.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: No forecast available.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: The occupation is mainly hands-on and workshop-based: manufacturing, adjusting, and repairing tools, plus milling, turning, spark erosion, and bench work. These core tasks require physical interaction with machines, materials, tolerances, and real-world troubleshooting, so AI cannot easily replace the central work. However, there is some moderate exposure through digital design, programming of machine tools, process planning, documentation, and quality control assistance, where AI can support drafting, optimization, and diagnostics. Overall, the job is mostly physical and technical rather than screen-based, so theoretical AI exposure is limited to moderate.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: For toolmakers (verktygsmakare), there are observable but still limited AI adoption signals as of early 2026. In Swedish manufacturing and machining, AI is mainly appearing through adjacent applications such as CNC programming assistance, predictive maintenance, inspection/quality-control vision systems, and CAM/process optimization rather than as a core day-to-day toolmaker workflow. Some employers are experimenting with AI-enabled machine monitoring, anomaly detection, and automated measurement/vision inspection, and these tools can support mold, die, and precision tool production. However, the occupation remains highly hands-on and craft/precision-based, with most work centered on manual fitting, grinding, spark erosion, setup, and repair tasks that are not yet broadly AI-driven. Overall this looks like early adoption: tools exist and some firms are testing or selectively using them, but there is no strong evidence of mainstream, widespread AI usage across the occupation.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: This occupation is primarily hands-on and craft-based: shaping, making, engraving, repairing, stone setting, and polishing precious-metal items require fine manual skill, tactile judgment, and direct interaction with materials. However, for the core occupational tasks in SSYK 7312—manual forming, engraving, repair, gemstone setting, and metal finishing—there is little evidence of mainstream AI deployment in everyday workshop practice as of early 2026. Most workplaces in this occupation remain heavily craft-based, with adoption limited to a few early experiments or small businesses using general-purpose tools rather than occupation-specific AI systems. So the right rating is early but weak adoption: 2.

descriptionEnglish: Diagnose faults, maintain and repair engines for motor vehicles and boats. Repair and service motorcycles, cars, lorries, articulated lorries, trailers, buses, tractors and bicycles. Maintain and repair agricultural, forestry, construction and earthmoving machinery. Order, stock and sell spare parts and accessories. Not including: aircraft mechanic (7232); helicopter mechanic (7232); locomotive repairer (7233); railway coach repairer (7233).

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that competition for jobs for motor vehicle mechanics and repairers will be very low over the next year. This means that people with the required education or experience will have very good opportunities for work, but the labour market may vary depending on specialization within the occupation.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: Over a two-year horizon, the Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that the opportunities for work for motor vehicle mechanics and repairers will be very good. Job prospects in the occupation improve if you have relevant certificates, authorizations, or occupational credentials.
forecast.ingress: There are about 33,900 employed motor vehicle mechanics and repairers in Sweden, of whom about 3 percent are women and 97 percent are men.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: This occupation is primarily hands-on and physically embodied: diagnosing faults on vehicles and machinery, performing maintenance and repairs, replacing parts, and working in workshops or field settings. AI can assist with diagnostics, troubleshooting, parts identification, service scheduling, and inventory management, but it cannot perform the core repair work or physical inspection itself. The job therefore has some cognitive/digital components, yet the main task structure remains mostly manual and context-dependent, so theoretical AI exposure is low to moderate.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Current AI adoption for motor vehicle mechanics and vehicle repairers appears marginal to early-stage rather than widespread. As of early 2026, there are observable signals of AI use in adjacent areas such as predictive maintenance, remote diagnostics, workshop scheduling, parts logistics, and connected-vehicle fault detection, but these are typically embedded in OEM platforms, fleet-management systems, or dealership tools rather than broadly used daily by mechanics across the occupation. In Sweden, some larger commercial vehicle dealers, truck workshops, agricultural machinery service networks, and premium automotive brands are likely experimenting with AI-assisted diagnostics and service planning, yet the occupation remains strongly hands-on and tool-driven, with most work still relying on standard diagnostic software, manuals, and technician expertise. There is no strong evidence of mainstream, occupation-wide AI tooling comparable to what is seen in software, customer support, or office-based analytical roles. Overall, the current adoption signal is limited and uneven, so a low-to-moderate score is appropriate.

nameEnglish: Metal working machine setters and operators
description:
Sköter, styr och övervakar hel- och halvautomatiska maskiner som bearbetar arbetsstycken av metall eller liknande material genom borrning, hyvling, svarvning, fräsning, gängning, slipning, svetsning m.m. Programmerar datorstyrda maskiner och korrigerar program. Sköter maskiner som bearbetar och skär material med hjälp av elektroner. Hanterar maskiner som formar detaljer i metall genom pressning eller tryck samt maskiner som kapar eller klipper metallprodukter. Sätter upp, utprovar och besiktigar verktyg. Ställer in och programmerar hel- och halvautomatiska verktygsmaskiner.

Hit hör inte
Slipare, metall (7224)
Svetsare, manuell (7212)
descriptionEnglish: Operate, set up and monitor fully and semi-automatic machines that machine metal workpieces or similar materials by drilling, planing, turning, milling, threading, grinding, welding and similar processes. Program computer-controlled machines and correct programs. Operate machines that process and cut materials using electrons. Handle machines that shape metal parts by pressing or punching, as well as machines that cut or shear metal products. Set up, test and inspect tools. Set and program fully and semi-automatic machine tools. Not including: grinder, metal (7224); manual welder (7212).

forecast.shortTermOutlook: The Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that competition for jobs for machine operators in manufacturing will be low over the next year. This means that people with the required education or experience will have very good opportunities for work, but the labour market may vary depending on specialization within the occupation.
forecast.mediumTermOutlook: Over a two-year horizon, the Swedish Public Employment Service assesses that the opportunities for work for machine operators in manufacturing will be good. Job prospects in the occupation improve if you have relevant certificates, authorizations, or occupational credentials.
forecast.ingress: The assessment for machine operators in manufacturing is an overall assessment covering machine operators in cement, stone and concrete products; machine operators in the meat and fish processing industry; machine operators in the plastics industry; operators in sawmills, planing mills and plywood, etc.; machine operators in chemical-technical and photographic products; machine operators in the paper products industry; other machine operators in the textile, leather and hide industry; machine operators in dairies; other machine operators in the food industry, etc.; machine operators in the rubber industry; machine operators in the flour, bakery and confectionery industry; machine operators in surface treatment; rolling-mill operators; machine setters and machine operators in metal work; machine carpenters and machine operators in the wood industry; machine operators in surface treatment of wood; machine operators in bleaching, dyeing and washing; machine operators in filling, packing and labelling; manual surface finishers in wood; and machine operators in pharmaceutical products. There are about 115,000 employed machine operators in manufacturing in Sweden, of whom about 21 percent are women and 79 percent are men.

scores.theoreticalExposureRationale: This occupation combines substantial physical machine operation with meaningful cognitive and digital tasks. Operators and setters program, adjust, test, and supervise semi- and fully automated metalworking machines, which gives AI some exposure through programming support, fault detection, parameter optimization, predictive maintenance, and quality-control assistance. However, the core work still requires hands-on setup, monitoring of real machines, tool fitting, inspection, and response to machine behavior in a physical production environment. Because the job is mixed rather than predominantly digital, and because many tasks remain embodied and site-specific, the exposure is moderate rather than high.
scores.currentAdoptionRationale: Current AI adoption in this occupation appears to be early-stage rather than mainstream. In Swedish manufacturing, there are observable signals of AI in CNC/industrial settings such as machine vision for quality control, predictive maintenance, anomaly detection, and some AI-assisted process optimization in advanced factories. However, for machine setters and operators in metalworking, day-to-day work is still dominated by conventional automation, PLC/CNC programming, manual setup, tool adjustment, and supervision rather than direct AI tooling. Job ads and vendor marketing show increasing interest in smart manufacturing and AI-enabled production systems, but there is limited evidence that AI is broadly embedded in routine operator workflows across the occupation. Overall, the signals suggest experimentation and selective deployment in larger or more advanced plants, not widespread adoption.

scores.currentAdoptionRationale:

The work is highly hands-on and physical: fabrication, repair, fitting, inspection, cleaning tanks, and working on vehicle sheet metal are still mainly done with manual tools, measurement equipment, and established workshop processes.

There are some adjacent AI-enabled tools in the broader maintenance and manufacturing environment, such as computer vision for quality inspection, predictive maintenance systems, and AI-assisted quoting/planning in workshops. However, these are not widespread enough to count as mainstream occupation-level adoption for tinsmiths specifically. Most current use is limited to isolated pilots or indirect support functions rather than day-to-day core work.

Overall, the observable signal is weak: a few digital/AI experiments exist in manufacturing and repair settings, but there is little evidence of broad employer adoption targeted at this occupation.
